agent_approach_summary: The agent correctly identified that Last.fm session keys were being stored in the global properties table with a user-ID prefix and began implementing the intended refactor: adding a user_props migration, defining a UserPropsRepository, wiring it into DataStore/persistence, and starting to update the LastFM code to use the new repository.
contamination_evidence: The staged F2P test change in core/agents/lastfm/agent_test.go writes through ds.UserProps(ctx).Put(sessionKeyProperty, "SK-1"), which requires an unexported package-level identifier named exactly sessionKeyProperty in the lastfm package. The problem statement never specifies that constant name, or even that the key string must be exposed as a constant in that package. A correct implementation could reasonably use a string literal, reuse a constant in model (the agent appears to have added LastFMSessionKey there), or choose a different internal name, and would still fail to compile/pass these tests. That is white-box coupling to gold-patch internals rather than behavior required by the spec.
reasoning: This task is not clean. The agent understood the requested architectural change and was moving in the right direction, but timed out before completing and validating the patch. More importantly, the F2P tests themselves are coupled to an internal implementation detail that is not derivable from the problem statement: they reference the exact unexported constant name sessionKeyProperty from the lastfm package. The spec says to move user-specific properties to a dedicated user_props table and provide a user-scoped repository like UserPropsRepository; it does not require a package-local constant with that exact name. So an agent could implement the described behavior correctly and still fail these tests purely بسبب a different internal naming choice. That makes this contamination via test coupling. I am not marking it CONFIRMED_SEVERE only because the trajectory ended in timeout before an actual near-correct patch was rejected, but the coupling in the staged test is direct and strong.
